[330:46]has been negotiating with the government in Venezuela and at one point during the year had actually reached a deal with Maduro and

[341:22]Trump's strategy its brinksmanship

[341:26]It's brinksmanship and its maximum pressure and this is what we've seen in North Korea in Syria in Iran in Russia

[341:32]And this is what we've seen in Venezuela also

[341:34]Which is you use a combination of soft power and gunboat diplomacy to increase pressure to the maximum level

[341:42]And so what does that mean?

[341:44]for example with

[341:45]North Korea

[341:47]We already had more sanctions than

[341:51]Then then we could even levy on North Korea. There weren't any more sanctions. We could apply

[341:55]How do we hurt them economically? We went to Beijing their only trading partner their biggest trading partner

[342:01]And we said China if you want to work with us, you need to help us

[342:06]Denuclearize the Korean Peninsula you need to put the screws in North Korea

[342:21]and said, my button is bigger and mine works. And you're gonna see fire and fury and the storm

[342:29]is coming. And the idea was to create, this is called brinksmanship. It's bringing them to the

[342:36]using maximum pressure, using that soft power,

[342:40]economic, diplomatic, psychological tactics,

[342:43]as well as gunboat diplomacy,

[342:45]bringing the visible presence of US force

[342:49]to bear on these countries, demonstrating it to them.

[342:53]And then offering them a choice.

[342:55]We can meet and have a détente,

[342:57]we can normalize relations,

[342:59]which they did with North Korea in disgust,

[343:01]or we can go to war.

[343:02]But I don't think that Trump actually wants all of these regime change wars.

[343:07]I don't think he actually wants invasions.

[343:09]This is the tactic.

[343:11]You see it in Iran also, you see it in Russia.

[343:14]It's these levers.

[343:16]It's calibrating your approach, calibrating your openness to diplomacy, signaling at various

[343:22]times and openness to negotiate and openness to meet and openness to talk or normalize,

[343:33]using the economic tools at your disposal

[343:35]to create destabilization, to create pain,

[343:40]stress on the regime, but also then to incite fear

[343:43]by again, bringing down the force,

[343:46]demonstrating the force capability of the United States.

[343:56]first he puts a $50 million bounty on Maduro's head.

[348:19]to act quickly and decisively, which I like, and he's able to do that for a period of time.

[348:27]the bureaucratic, the democratic state catches up to him with all their rules and all their oversight and everything and

[348:34]that's what it looks like is happening here.

[348:37]So, that's a situation as it stands in Venezuela.

[348:40]I've been very clear about this from the beginning.

[348:42]We can maintain at once that Venezuela is part of our legitimate sphere of influence.

[348:49]People are always saying, why don't we focus on the national security threats at home?

[348:54]What does Iran have to do with us?

[348:55]And I totally agree with that.

[348:57]And that's a salient argument to make when people are talking about war in Yemen, in

[349:05]In these types of countries, in Ukraine even, but Venezuela is in the Western hemisphere.

[349:12]It's right across the Gulf of Mexico.

[349:13]It's not far.

[349:15]And I would consider both North and South America are legitimate sphere of influence.

[349:21]Anything that happens there could be threatening to the United States.

[349:29]We are protected from invasion.

[349:31]We are protected from non nuclear powers that don't have a strategic air force or strategic

[349:36]nuclear capability.

[349:38]We're protected from them by distance because of the oceans.

[349:43]Iran can't hurt us because we're separated by an ocean.

[349:46]They don't have missiles that can hit us.

[349:48]They don't have an air force that can hit us.

[349:49]They can't threaten us.

[349:51]They can't invade us.

[349:58]But that is not the case with Central and South America.

[350:02]Those countries, they could theoretically pose a threat to the United States because

[350:07]they're not separated from us by an ocean.

[350:10]They're connected to our country by land and by a not insignificant, or rather, or what

[350:17]is the right wording for that, by a pretty insignificant distance over water too, the

[350:26]So people like to say, well, we should stay out of it. I disagree. I think this is our business.

[350:32]And when you look at what the Maduro regime has done, forget about the fact that he's socialist.

[350:36]I don't really care about that. Who really cares? It's his country. They have national sovereignty.

[350:42]What I care about is the fact that he is aligned with US adversaries.

[350:46]And maybe if it was one country, it wouldn't be a threat, but he's allied with Cuba, Nicaragua, China, Russia, Iran.

[350:55]And to the extent that they are working with Iran's military, to the extent that they are

[351:00]working with China and Russia, and they get Russian weapons and they get Chinese investment.

[351:06]We are fighting a new Cold War, a new Monroe doctrine in South America.

[351:12]We're trying to expel China from these Latin American countries.

[351:16]So why are we going to kick out the Chinese in Argentina and try to kick them out in Brazil

[351:21]and in all these other places, but we're going to let them have a foothold in Venezuela?

[351:26]Dual use infrastructure, they're going to have the oil there. They're going to control

[351:30]the gold there. They might build drones there. Why would we let them do this? We're going

[351:34]to be at war with them. We're going to let them exist across the pond like that. I don't

[351:38]think so.

[351:39]So, at the minimum, it is in our national security interests that we should not allow

[351:44]Venezuela to take the side of U.S. strategic rivals, which is what China and Russia are.

[351:51]And Maduro has very conscientiously created an axis with them.

[351:55]He's in league with them.

[351:57]And that's not to say that he's going to invade America or attack America, but theoretically

[352:07]or be used against the United States in some way.

[352:10]And I don't know why we would tolerate that.

[352:13]So I am in favor of US intervention in Venezuela,

[352:17]although not necessarily regime change.

[352:20]I believe that America has strategic interests.

[352:24]That doesn't mean that it doesn't matter

[352:26]how we pursue them.

[352:27]I still think that we should pursue

[352:28]a reasonable and restrained foreign policy,

[352:32]the policy of restraint.

[352:35]I'm in favor of that.

[352:36]So I don't think we should go in guns blazing and take out Maduro and then all these other

[352:40]countries.

[352:41]But I do think that this is the right strategy, maximum pressure, a negotiated settlement.

[352:48]All we want is the oil, the gold.

[352:50]We want your allegiance and we want Maduro out.

[352:52]And that's it.

[352:54]And you could be a Bolivarian socialist republic.

[352:57]I really don't care.

[352:58]And I don't even think we need to kill the guy.

[353:02]I don't think that would be good for the United States to destabilize this country.

[353:06]You're going to get another exodus.

[353:07]You might get guerrilla warfare.

[353:09]It might bleed over into these other states and create a security challenge for Brazil,

[353:13]for Colombia, for Guyana, certainly.

[353:16]And by the way, there's a lot of unexploded resources right there in Guyana and the Esa

[353:26]So, I definitely fall on the side of Rick Grinnell on this one.

[353:30]We should have a negotiated settlement.

[353:32]Let's get the allegiance of the government there.

[353:35]Let's get access to their raw materials.

[353:38]If we can go and think about it this way, people say, how does that benefit us?

[353:42]If we could go into Venezuela, we can make their oil production efficient.

[353:47]Their oil production is less than half of what it should be because of sanctions, because

[353:54]If we got down to Venezuela and we started pumping their oil,

[353:58]that would be a huge energy boon to the United States,

[354:01]and we would get all of it.

[354:02]Global oil prices would fall.

[354:04]That would hurt Russia.

[354:05]It would be beneficial for American consumers.

[354:08]We would secure the energy for our increasing electricity needs.

[354:11]And not only that, if we have a stable government

[354:14]in Venezuela that we're allied with,

[354:16]we can then extract all the energy that's in Guyana also,
